evals.prompt: Leadership wants us on microservices by Q3. We're a team of nine on a Django monolith, ~120k lines, deploys weekly, occasional slow reports but nothing on fire. I've been asked to write up the migration plan. Where do I start?
evals.expected_output: Should refuse to start with a migration plan and start with architecture characteristics instead — nothing about the decision is answerable until the top three are named, and if nobody can name the characteristic that disqualifies a modular monolith, it isn't disqualified. Should note that microservices rates ★ on cost, simplicity, and performance/responsiveness, and that a nine-person team is at or below the size where a single bounded context is appropriate at all. Should land on a recommendation of a modular monolith or service-based architecture as the honest interim, presenting service-based explicitly as the designed stepping stone: decompose further later from a position of knowledge. Should mention that decoupling has levels (source → deployment → service) and to take the cheapest that works, pushing the decision as late as possible. Should recommend fixing module boundaries inside the monolith first with enforced coupling caps and forbidden-dependency rules in CI, since that work is required for any future split and is not wasted. Should name resume-driven architecture / architecture by implication as the risk without being condescending about leadership. May suggest an ADR capturing the decision with the justification. Should NOT produce a service decomposition plan, and should NOT simply agree that a Q3 microservices migration is achievable.

evals.prompt: We split our platform into 6 microservices last year. They all talk to the same Postgres instance (different schemas though). Deploys are still coordinated because schema changes break things. Someone said we're not really doing microservices — is that just semantics?
evals.expected_output: Should say it is not semantics and give the precise reason: the database is inside the architecture quantum, so six services on one Postgres instance are one quantum regardless of schema separation. Should spell out the consequences — one deployment risk, one scaling ceiling, one failure domain — and name this as a distributed monolith: paying microservices' costs (network latency, distributed debugging, operational overhead) while receiving the monolith's constraints. Should identify coordinated deploys as the observable proof, not an incidental annoyance. Should offer two honest paths rather than assuming one: either give each service its own data and remove synchronous required-path calls, or merge back to service-based architecture and stop calling it microservices — and should present merging back as a legitimate, under-used move rather than a failure. If keeping the shared database, should mention logically partitioning it with per-domain entity libraries and locking common entity objects, and note that a single all-entities shared library is the antipattern producing exactly these coordinated deploys. Should NOT say the split was worthless, and should NOT recommend microservices-with-shared-database as a stable end state.

evals.prompt: Our `Order` entity has TypeORM decorators on it, a `@ManyToOne` to `Customer`, and a couple of methods that call `this.customer.creditLimit`. It also has `createdAt` set with `new Date()` in the constructor. Reviewer flagged it as 'not clean architecture' but didn't say what to do. Is it actually a problem?
evals.expected_output: Should identify distinct problems rather than a general appeal to cleanliness. (1) ORM decorators are a Dependency Rule violation at the worst layer — a framework upgrade now forces changes to business rules and the domain cannot be tested without the ORM; fix by moving mapping outside the entity, not by relocating the entity. Should apply Noback's two rules and note that an annotated entity is still core code with a Rule 1 violation. (2) `new Date()` in the constructor is a hidden dependency on the system clock — replace with an injected clock or, better, pass the time as an argument since 'the current time' is contextual data. (3) The `@ManyToOne` object reference to `Customer` crosses an aggregate boundary — aggregates should reference each other by identity, not object reference, and `this.customer.creditLimit` means the invariant now spans two aggregates. Should ask whether the credit-limit rule is a true invariant by saying it aloud in the ubiquitous language, and if it is not this user's job to keep consistent, use eventual consistency. Should be proportionate — this is fixable incrementally, not a rewrite — and may note the legacy rule of improving a little every day. Should NOT recommend adding an interface for the entity, and should NOT claim the entity belongs in the infrastructure layer.

evals.prompt: Doing a review of an internal tool. It's layered — controller → service → repository — but honestly about 4 out of 5 endpoints are just controller calls service calls repository with zero logic in the middle. Team wants to let controllers hit repositories directly for the simple cases. Reasonable?
evals.expected_output: Should name this as the architecture sinkhole antipattern and give the actual threshold: up to ~20% of requests passing through is normal and acceptable, but 80% means the style itself is wrong — this is not a case of a few unnecessary layers. Should push back on the proposed fix specifically: letting controllers hit repositories directly is the Fast-Lane Reader, which fixes throughput and destroys layers of isolation, so the persistence layer is no longer replaceable and change management gets much harder. Should offer the better options — either move to a different style (and note this may be a genuine CRUD tool that doesn't deserve decoupling at all, per Noback's 'should this project be decoupled?' test: something generatable from a config file does not), or deliberately open specific layers while documenting which are open, which are closed, and why. Should stress that the open/closed decision must be documented and enforced, not left implicit. Should treat 'it's an internal tool' as relevant evidence rather than dismissing it — the effort may genuinely outweigh the benefit here. Should NOT reflexively defend the layers, and should NOT approve ad-hoc bypassing without naming which layers are open.

evals.prompt: We're breaking up our monolith and I've drafted the service boundaries: auth-service, notification-service, database-service, file-service, and api-service. Each one owns a technical capability so there's no duplication. Sanity check before I socialise this?
evals.expected_output: Should identify that these boundaries are drawn on technical capability rather than domain, and explain the consequence concretely: every business feature will cut across every service, so a single change means coordinated edits and deploys across four of the five. Should offer the falsification test — a cross-cutting feature that touches every service proves the boundary was drawn in the wrong dimension — and note that heavy inter-service chatter is the same diagnosis. Should flag `database-service` specifically as both an entity-trap-adjacent construct and a guarantee of a shared quantum. Should recommend domain partitioning instead: identify subdomains, classify them as core, supporting, or generic, and draw bounded contexts sized to roughly one team of under ten. Should note that auth and notification are likely generic subdomains that should be bought or outsourced rather than built, and that misclassifying generic as core is the most expensive mistake available. Should explicitly address the 'no duplication' motivation: reuse is coupling, and wanting maximum decoupling and maximum reuse simultaneously is incompatible — reuse plumbing, never the domain, and some duplication is the price of the architecture. Should mention Conway's Law / the Inverse Conway Maneuver if team structure would fight the new boundaries. Should NOT simply approve the list.

evals.prompt: Placing an order has to reserve inventory, charge the card, and create the shipment. Those are three services. We've been looking at 2PC vs a saga library. Which way should we go?
evals.expected_output: Should reframe before answering: 'we need a distributed transaction' means the granularity is too fine, and granularity should be fixed before reaching for either option. Should explain why a saga is genuinely expensive rather than just complex — it creates connascence of values across network boundaries, the second-strongest dynamic connascence, plus heavy coordination traffic and requests sitting in a pending state — and state the rule that if transactions are a dominant feature of the system, microservices was the wrong style. Should apply the whose-job-is-it test per step: if it is this user's job to make the data consistent, try transactional; if it is another user's or the system's job, use eventual consistency, publish a domain event, and negotiate the acceptable delay with domain experts, who tolerate far more than developers expect. Should note that charging a card is very likely a different consistency requirement from creating a shipment, so this is not one decision but three. Should recommend first merging the services that share the invariant — likely order and inventory reservation — and only then using a saga with compensating transactions if boundaries genuinely cannot move. Should mention that one aggregate is modified per transaction, and that failing that indicates a wrong boundary or a missing concept. Should treat 2PC as the worse option and say why briefly. Should NOT open by comparing saga libraries.

evals.prompt: We're integrating a third-party shipping provider. Their API returns things like `ShipmentQuoteResponseV2` with `carrier_code`, `svc_lvl`, `rate_cents`. My plan is to extract an interface from their SDK client so we can swap providers later, and use their response types directly since they're already well-structured. Fine?
evals.expected_output: Should identify that extracting an interface from the SDK client achieves nothing — it is step one of the two-step abstraction recipe without step two, so it buys indirection with no decoupling and the vendor's vocabulary survives into the domain. Should apply the test 'would this interface still be useful if the implementation changed radically?' rather than 'can I mock it?', which is true of every interface including bad ones. Should name the specific tells: a return type shaped like the vendor's (`ShipmentQuoteResponseV2`), field names that are the vendor's (`svc_lvl`), and method names matching endpoints. Should prescribe two layers — a façade whose methods mirror the vendor's endpoints with the API key as a constructor argument, and above it an interface phrased in the domain's own words (something like `ShippingRates.quoteFor(shipment): Money`) whose implementation consumes the façade. Should name the upper layer as an anticorruption layer, note it should be used any time a foreign representation would enter the model, and that it pays for itself the first time the upstream changes. Should recommend owning the value objects rather than exposing vendor types. May note the escalation ladder for testing an external service and that a contract test belongs on the port. Should NOT approve using vendor response types in the domain because they are 'well-structured'.

evals.prompt: Product is insisting on 99.999% availability for the whole platform. It's in the draft contract. I think it's unrealistic but 'that's expensive' hasn't landed. How do I push back without sounding obstructive?
evals.expected_output: Should lead with the translation move rather than with cost: 99.999% is 5 minutes 35 seconds of downtime per year, about 1 second per day. Should explain why this works — it converts an ego argument into an arithmetic one, and people concede to arithmetic — and should supply the surrounding table so 99.9% (8 hrs 46 min/year, 86 sec/day) is visible as the alternative. Should give the ordered negotiation sequence: read the buzzword for the real characteristic, gather information first (what is the current measured availability, what does a deploy cost in downtime today), translate into concrete units, then divide and conquer — five nines almost never applies to the whole platform, only to one critical path, and scoping it down collapses the cost. Should state explicitly that qualified cost and time come LAST, because leading with cost poisons the negotiation and reads as refusal. Should mention that the requirement needs to become measurable to be governable at all, and suggest a fitness function plus an SLO rather than an SLA where possible, noting that an SLA is usually legally binding and an SLO usually is not — relevant given it is in a contract. May mention demonstration defeats discussion (show the current numbers). Should NOT frame this as product being wrong, and should NOT lead with the architecture style implications.

evals.prompt: Starting a new B2B SaaS — basically CRUD over accounts, contacts, and deals, plus a dashboard. I want to build it right from day one: event sourcing for the audit trail, CQRS so reads scale, and microservices per aggregate. Better to do it now than retrofit. Thoughts?
evals.expected_output: Should decline the plan and give reasons rather than a taste objection, addressing each piece separately. On event sourcing: it is a separate and much larger commitment than CQRS, only pays off on complex competitive-advantage models, drags CQRS in behind it because event streams are hard to query, and an audit trail is a far cheaper problem — an append-only audit log or domain events persisted alongside the model solves it without the model becoming the log. On CQRS: adopt only after repository finders and DTOs have genuinely failed a UI cutting across many aggregate types, or when read/write volumes sharply diverge; adopted speculatively it removes no real risk and adds accidental complexity, and a use-case-shaped read model built directly from the data source in one query is the cheap default. On microservices per aggregate: that is grains of sand, guaranteeing distributed transactions for ordinary use cases, and microservices rates ★ on cost, simplicity, and performance. Should note that no architecture characteristics have been named, so none of these choices can be justified yet, and that designing to support everything is the generic architecture antipattern. Should also raise Vernon's DDD scorecard threshold and observe that CRUD over accounts/contacts/deals may not clear the bar for DDD at all — and per Noback, something genuinely generatable may not deserve decoupling. Should offer the constructive alternative: a modular monolith with enforced module boundaries, which preserves every option and is the cheap version of 'build it right'. Should invoke that good architecture maximises the number of decisions NOT made, and the last responsible moment. Should NOT propose a compromise stack of two of the three, and should NOT agree that retrofitting is harder than removing speculative complexity.

evals.prompt: We agreed on a layered architecture with a strict rule that the domain must not depend on infrastructure. Six months in, half the domain imports the ORM and someone put an HTTP client in a value object. We have a wiki page and a diagram. How do we actually make this stick?
evals.expected_output: Should state the core rule plainly: only the compiler and the build enforce architecture — a wiki page and a diagram document intent and constrain nothing, so the outcome was predictable. Should prescribe fitness functions wired into the continuous build, naming concrete tooling appropriate to enforcement (ArchUnit, NetArchTest, PyTestArch, TSArch, deptrac, JDepend) and concrete rules: forbidden imports from domain to infrastructure, cycle detection, a coupling-point cap per module, complexity thresholds. Should stress the rule that makes them work: explain every fitness function before imposing it, because an ivory-tower rule breeds workarounds — suppressions, wrapper classes, carve-outs — rather than compliance, which leaves you with false confidence on top of the original problem. Should recommend an ADR capturing the decision WITH its justification, noting that a rule whose why was never given gets re-litigated forever (Groundhog Day) and that the team currently has no defence except authority. Should be practical about the existing violations: baseline the current state so the build goes green, then ratchet, rather than blocking all work on a big-bang cleanup. Should mention access modifiers and module boundaries as structural enforcement alongside the CI checks, and may suggest risk storming or periodic review to catch decay in things no rule covers. Should NOT recommend better documentation, a stricter code review policy, or a training session as the primary fix.
